1.pericope_label: Oh That My Head Were Waters
1.synthesis: Chapter 9 opens with Jeremiah's most extended personal lament: the wish for supernatural weeping capacity — “Oh that my head were a spring of water and my eyes a fountain of tears, so that I could weep day and night for the slain of my dear people!” (v.1). But immediately follows the wish for escape: “Oh that I had a lodging place for travellers in the wilderness, so that I could leave my people and go away from them!” (v.2) — because they are all adulterers and treacherous deceivers. The social fabric has been utterly destroyed by universal deceit: every brother is a Jacob (a supplanter), every neighbour goes spreading slander (v.4). They have trained their tongues to lie (v.5). YHWH will refine them in affliction — what else can he do with his sinful people? (v.7). The tongue is described as a “lethal arrow” — peace spoken with the mouth while ambush is planned in the heart (v.8).
1.historical_context: The twin desires — to weep endlessly and to escape entirely — define the impossible position of the prophet of judgment: he loves the people he must condemn and must announce the destruction of those he weeps for. The “every brother is a Jacob” (kol-ʾāḥ ʿāqôb yĕʿāqēb, v.4) is a Hebrew wordplay: Jacob's name means “he supplants/deceives,” and the root recurs throughout the Genesis narrative. To call every brother a Jacob is to say that the social world has become a replay of the family dynamics at their worst — manipulation, deception, and self-advancement at others' expense. The YHWH-refining question of v.7 (“What else can I do?”) is one of the most anthropomorphic statements in Jeremiah: the divine query expresses genuine pathos at the lack of options.
1.literary_structure: The section is constructed around two polarities: the desire to weep (v.1) and the desire to flee (v.2), between which the social indictment (vv.3–8) explains why both are simultaneously true. The social portrait is built by accumulation: every brother, every neighbour, they have trained their tongues, they weary themselves with sin (v.5), they dwell in the midst of deception (v.6). The tongue-as-lethal-arrow image (v.8) closes the section with the sharp image of weaponised speech — a bow drawn by the tongue, aimed through pleasant words at the heart of the neighbour.
1.theological_application: The simultaneous desire to weep and to escape captures the prophetic condition at its most honest: compassion does not require the prophet to remain unmoved by the community's rejection, and genuine love can coexist with the desire for distance from those who exhaust and reject you. The social-trust collapse (vv.4–8) is a diagnostic of what covenant violation produces at the communal level: when YHWH is not the common reference point, mutual accountability disappears and the social fabric unravels into universal deception. The refining question (v.7) suggests that affliction is YHWH's last available instrument when the normal means of correction have been exhausted.
1.christology: Jeremiah's weeping over the slain of his people (v.1) is the prototype of the tears of Jesus over Jerusalem (John 11:35; Luke 19:41) — the weeping prophet figure that reaches its fulfilment in Christ. The “every brother is a Jacob” indictment of universal deception finds its solution in Christ as the true Israel, the one who overcomes deception with truth, the one who — unlike Jacob — wins his blessing without supplanting his brother. The lethal tongue-arrow (v.8) is the human social reality that Christ's words of grace and truth counter: “If you hold to my teaching, you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free” (John 8:32).

10.synthesis: YHWH himself takes up the lament: weeping and wailing for the mountains, lamentation for the wilderness pastures, because they have been laid waste (v.10). Jerusalem will become a heap of rubble and a haunt of jackals; the cities of Judah a wasteland (v.11). The wisdom-challenge: who is wise enough to understand this? Who has received the word from the LORD and can explain it? (v.12). The answer: because they abandoned YHWH's Torah and followed the stubbornness of their hearts after the Baals (vv.13–14). YHWH will feed this people wormwood and give them poisoned water (v.15). He will scatter them among unknown nations and pursue them with the sword until he has consumed them (v.16).
10.historical_context: The ecological mourning (v.10: mountains and wilderness pastures weeping) reflects the prophetic tradition that land and people are bound together in covenant consequence — the land suffers with its inhabitants when covenant is violated. The wisdom-challenge (v.12: who is wise enough to understand this?) is a formal invitation to the scribal wisdom class to apply their interpretive skills to the covenant: the explanation YHWH then provides (vv.13–14) is the standard Deuteronomic analysis — Torah abandonment, Baal-following. Wormwood as a judgment metaphor (v.15) appears also in Amos 5:7 and Lamentations 3:15.
10.literary_structure: The section moves from divine lament (vv.10–11), through the wisdom-challenge (v.12), the covenant-mechanism explanation (vv.13–14), to the judgment announcement (vv.15–16). The divine weeping for the mountains (v.10) is one of the most striking theophanic mourning passages: YHWH himself takes up a lament over the land his people have destroyed. The jackal-haunt image (v.11: mĕʿôn tannîm) is a standard desolation-formula that recurs throughout Jeremiah and connects the historical desolation to the chaos-imagery of the uninhabited wilderness.
10.theological_application: The wisdom-challenge (v.12) — “Who is wise enough to understand this?” — followed by YHWH's own explanation models the biblical understanding of wisdom as primarily theological interpretation, not technical expertise. The explanation is not complex: Torah abandonment and Baal-following. But the wisdom to see this clearly and draw its consequences requires divine revelation, not merely observation. YHWH's weeping for the land (v.10) is an important pastoral image: the God who pronounces the judgment also mourns its consequences, not indifferently but with genuine grief over what the covenant community's rebellion has cost both them and the created world they were meant to steward.
10.christology: The wormwood and poisoned water (v.15) become images of eschatological judgment in Revelation 8:11, where the star called Wormwood falls on the waters at the third trumpet. The scattering among unknown nations (v.16) is the exile diaspora that becomes, providentially, the network through which the gospel spreads: the very scattering that was judgment becomes the mechanism of mission. Paul's missionary strategy of following the synagogues of the Jewish diaspora (the scattered people of Jeremiah's judgment) is the divine reversal of v.16 — the sword of judgment replaced by the word of life.

17.synthesis: YHWH summons the professional mourning women (hamĕqônenôt) — the skilled lament-specialists of the ancient world — to come and raise a dirge over the dying land (vv.17–18). Their lament is voiced in prophetic anticipation: “We are devastated! We are utterly disgraced — because we have had to leave the land!” (v.19). YHWH then commands the women directly to teach their daughters to mourn and their neighbour to raise a dirge (v.20), because death has climbed through the windows like an intruder, cutting down children in the streets and young men in the squares (v.21). Human corpses will lie like manure on the field, like grain-stalks behind the reaper, with no one to gather them (v.22).
17.historical_context: Professional mourning women (hamĕqônenôt) were a recognised social institution in the ancient Near East: specialists in the lamentation arts, trained to lead communal grief rituals. The summons to teach daughters to mourn (v.20) suggests that the need for mourning will be so extensive and persistent that it must be passed on to the next generation as a survival skill. The personification of death climbing through windows (v.21) invokes the ancient motif of death as an uninvited household intruder — a figure attested in Ugaritic mythology (where Mot/death enters through windows). The corpse-as-manure and corpse-as-grain-stalks images (v.22) emphasise the waste and abandonment of the unburied dead.
17.literary_structure: The section frames the mourning-commission around two divine commands: summon the mourning women (v.17) and teach your daughters to mourn (v.20). Between them, the lament content is voiced in the community's own words (vv.18–19). The death-intruder image (v.21) is the most vivid element: death entering through windows is not a battle-death but the death of ordinary life interrupted — children die in the streets, not on a battlefield. The grain-stalk image (v.22) closes with a harvest-of-death metaphor that inverts the harvest-of-grain: instead of gathered grain, scattered human bodies.
17.theological_application: The command to teach daughters to mourn (v.20) models a counter-cultural wisdom: in a world that suppresses or avoids grief, the community is called to develop the capacity for genuine lament as a communal competence. The mourning women's skilled lament preserves the emotional and social space for processing catastrophic loss together. The death-intrusion image (v.21) refuses to sanitise the consequences of covenant violation: it names the horror specifically — children killed in the streets — rather than abstracting it into policy language. This commitment to naming human suffering concretely is a feature of prophetic speech that pastoral care must emulate.
17.christology: The summons to mourning women and the teaching of daughters to lament finds its New Testament counterpart in Jesus' words to the daughters of Jerusalem on the way to the cross: “Do not weep for me; weep for yourselves and for your children” (Luke 23:28). Jesus himself becomes the occasion for the mourning that Jeremiah announced — and yet his resurrection transforms the mourning into joy. The church's lament tradition (psalms of lament, funeral liturgy, grief practices) stands in direct continuity with the mourning-women tradition Jeremiah here commands to be preserved and transmitted.

23.synthesis: In one of the most memorable wisdom-distillations in the prophets, YHWH declares: “Let not the wise boast in their wisdom, nor the mighty in their strength, nor the rich in their wealth; but let him who boasts boast in this — that he understands and knows me, that I am the LORD who exercises steadfast love, justice, and righteousness in the earth” (vv.23–24). YHWH then extends judgment beyond ethnic boundaries: physically circumcised nations and uncircumcised-in-heart Israel will share the same fate (v.25). The list of nations — Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and those who clip their temples — is encompassed in a single verdict: all are uncircumcised in heart (v.26).
23.historical_context: The three boasting prohibitions — wisdom, might, wealth — are the standard triads of ANE achievement prestige. Isaiah 29:14 targets the wisdom of the wise; Amos 2:14–15 targets military might; Ezekiel targets wealth. Jeremiah synthesises all three into a single prohibition. The contrast is not “do not pursue wisdom, strength, or wealth” but “do not make these the ground of your boast” — the self-defining, self-justifying confidence that these things make one secure. The three divine character qualities — ḥesed (steadfast love), mišpāṭ (justice), ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) — are YHWH's own resume, the only ground of authentic boast.
23.literary_structure: The section has two parts: the wisdom-oracle (vv.23–24) and the circumcision-judgment (vv.25–26). The connection is the consistent theme of claimed-status that does not correspond to reality: the wise, mighty, and rich claim status on false grounds; the circumcised nations (including Judah) claim covenant status on the false ground of physical rite while remaining uncircumcised in heart. Both forms of false boasting share the same problem: the status claimed is external and disconnected from the internal reality that would justify it.
23.theological_application: Paul quotes v.24 in 1 Corinthians 1:31 and 2 Corinthians 10:17 as the principle that governs the gospel: the cross dismantles every form of human boasting (wisdom, power, noble birth) and reconstitutes the only legitimate boast as knowing and being known by YHWH. The heart-circumcision argument of vv.25–26 anticipates the New Testament critique of ethnic religious privilege: external rite does not constitute covenant standing; only the transformation of heart by the Spirit produces the circumcision that matters (Romans 2:29).
23.christology: Paul's famous christologising of v.24 — “let him who boasts, boast in the Lord” (1 Corinthians 1:31) — makes Christ himself the content of the legitimate boast: knowing and being known by the LORD has been concretised as union with the crucified and risen Son. The three divine character qualities — steadfast love, justice, righteousness — are embodied in Christ: his ḥesed goes to the cross; his mišpāṭ condemns sin in the flesh; his ṣĕdāqāh is credited to those who are united with him by faith (Romans 3:26). The heart-circumcision of v.26 is performed by the Spirit of Christ in the act of new birth (Colossians 2:11).
